C overstates the nonfiction result by asserting no inference improvement at all, whereas the conclusion concerns whether the inference gain exceeds the recall gain, not whether it is zero. D reports a true post-period between-group gap but reframes the comparison as inference versus recall levels rather than as the change in inference relative to the change in recall.

targetedAbility: Choose the table reading showing the city with the bike-share program had a larger drop in short car trips than the comparison city, supporting the program-effect conclusion.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the table that support the planner's conclusion?
passage: An urban planner examined whether a new bike-share program reduced short car trips. He recorded the number of short car trips per resident in the city that launched the program and in a similar comparison city without one, both before and after the program's launch. He concluded that the bike-share program reduced short car trips in the city that adopted it.
stimulusTableHtml:
Short Car Trips per Resident Before and After Bike-Share Launch
CityBefore launchAfter launch
Program city4836
Comparison city4645

choices.text: Short car trips per resident decreased in the program city after the launch, while in the comparison city they stayed about the same.
Short car trips per resident decreased in the comparison city after the launch, while in the program city they stayed about the same.
Before the launch, the program city had more short car trips per resident than the comparison city.
After the launch, both the program city and the comparison city had fewer than 50 short car trips per resident.

explanation: The conclusion attributes a reduction to the program, so support must show the program city's trips falling while the comparison city's did not. Choice A shows this contrast. B reverses which city declined. C describes only the pre-launch baseline and shows no effect. D notes a shared post-launch property without comparing changes.

targetedAbility: Identify the data description showing the coating's corrosion-reducing benefit grew larger as salt concentration rose, supporting that protection held and even strengthened under the harshest conditions.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the graph that support the engineers' conclusion?
passage: Engineers seeking durable materials for coastal bridges tested a new polymer coating intended to slow corrosion, a problem that intensifies in salt-laden environments where conventional protections often fail first. They submerged coated and uncoated steel samples in solutions of three increasing salt concentrations and, after a fixed exposure period, measured the mass each sample lost to corrosion, in milligrams. Acknowledging that corrosion worsened for every sample as salinity climbed, the engineers nonetheless concluded that the coating's protective benefit did not erode under harsher conditions but instead became more pronounced, because the mass the coating spared each sample grew larger at higher salt concentrations.

choices.text: The difference in mass lost between the uncoated and coated samples was larger at the highest salt concentration than at the lowest salt concentration.
At each of the three salt concentrations, the coated steel sample lost less mass to corrosion than the uncoated steel sample lost at that same concentration.
Both the coated and the uncoated samples lost more mass to corrosion at the highest salt concentration than either lost at the lowest salt concentration.
At the highest salt concentration, the coated steel sample lost less than half as much mass to corrosion as the uncoated steel sample lost at that concentration.

explanation: The conclusion is that the coating's benefit became more pronounced as conditions harshened, so support must show the mass spared by the coating, that is, the gap between uncoated and coated loss, growing with salt concentration. Choice A states exactly this: the difference widens from 25 mg at Low to 60 mg at High. B is true and shows the coating helped at every concentration, but a constant per-concentration benefit is not the same as a growing one, so it supports protection without supporting the strengthening claim. C is true but describes only that corrosion rose with salinity for both samples, omitting the coating's effect entirely. D is true for the highest concentration alone and frames the benefit as a single ratio, which cannot show that the benefit increased relative to lower concentrations.

targetedAbility: Select the data reading showing the shaded coral had a higher survival rate than the unshaded coral, supporting the shading-benefit conclusion.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the graph that support the biologist's conclusion?
passage: A marine biologist tested whether providing artificial shade could help coral survive a period of unusually warm water. She measured the percentage of coral colonies that survived in a shaded section of reef and in an unshaded section after a summer heat wave. From her measurements, she concluded that shading improved coral survival during the heat wave.

choices.text: A higher percentage of coral colonies survived in the shaded section than in the unshaded section.
A higher percentage of coral colonies survived in the unshaded section than in the shaded section.
More than half of the coral colonies in the shaded section survived the heat wave.
Some coral colonies survived in both the shaded section and the unshaded section.

explanation: The conclusion is that shading improved survival, so support must show a higher survival percentage in the shaded section. Choice A states this. B reverses the relationship. C gives only the shaded value without comparing it to the unshaded section. D is true but makes no comparison.

targetedAbility: Select the description of the bar graph showing both reef sections gained coral cover after restoration but the sheltered section gained more, supporting the conclusion that shelter amplifies recovery.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the graph that support the researchers' conclusion?
passage: Coral restoration often involves transplanting laboratory-grown coral fragments onto degraded reefs, but recovery rates may depend heavily on local conditions. Marine biologist Eduardo Salgado and colleagues transplanted equal numbers of fragments onto two adjacent sections of a Caribbean reef—one exposed to strong wave action and one sheltered behind a natural rock barrier—and measured percentage live coral cover before transplantation and again two years afterward. Although both sections showed measurable improvement, the researchers concluded that physical shelter from wave action substantially amplifies the benefit of transplantation rather than merely permitting recovery to occur at all.

choices.text: In the exposed section, live coral cover rose from 12% to 19%, an increase of 7 percentage points, whereas in the sheltered section it rose from 14% to 38%, an increase of 24 percentage points.

Before transplantation, the sheltered section had 14% live coral cover while the exposed section had 12%, indicating that the sheltered section was already in better condition at the start of the study.
After transplantation, both the exposed section and the sheltered section showed measurable increases in live coral cover, rising to 19% and 38% respectively over the two-year period.
In the exposed section, live coral cover rose from 12% to 19%, whereas in the sheltered section it rose only from 14% to 38%, a smaller relative change given its higher starting point.

explanation: The conclusion is that shelter amplifies the benefit of transplantation, not merely that recovery occurs. Choice A is correct because it reports both sections' gains and shows the sheltered section's gain (24 points) far exceeded the exposed section's gain (7 points), directly supporting amplification. Choice B reports only starting values and addresses initial condition, not the effect of transplantation. Choice C is true but reports only that both increased, supporting that recovery occurred rather than that shelter amplified it. Choice D misstates the comparison, calling the larger sheltered gain 'smaller' and reversing the relationship.

targetedAbility: Select the graph description showing that the city with the new bike lanes had a larger drop in average commute time, supporting the planner's conclusion.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the graph that support the planner's conclusion?
passage: A transportation planner studied average morning commute times in two comparable mid-sized cities over a five-year period. One city, Marwick, installed an extensive network of protected bike lanes during the study, while the other city, Holloway, made no such changes. The planner concluded that the introduction of protected bike lanes in Marwick was associated with a greater reduction in average commute time than occurred in Holloway over the same period.

choices.text: In Marwick, average commute time fell from 31 minutes to 24 minutes, a reduction of 7 minutes, while in Holloway it fell from 29 minutes to 27 minutes, a reduction of 2 minutes.
In Marwick, average commute time fell from 31 minutes to 24 minutes over the five-year period, indicating that commutes there became shorter.
At the end of the study, Marwick had a shorter average commute time of 24 minutes than Holloway, which had an average commute time of 27 minutes.
In Holloway, average commute time fell from 29 minutes to 27 minutes, a smaller reduction than in Marwick despite Holloway starting with a shorter commute time.

explanation: The conclusion compares the size of the reduction in each city. Choice A correctly reports both reductions—7 minutes in Marwick versus 2 minutes in Holloway—showing Marwick's was greater. Choice B reports only Marwick's change and omits the comparison with Holloway. Choice C compares final values, not the reductions. Choice D introduces Holloway's lower starting point, which is irrelevant to comparing the magnitudes of reduction.

targetedAbility: Select the table description showing that the enriched-environment group both learned faster and retained more, supporting the dual conclusion about environmental enrichment.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the table that support Petrov's argument?
passage: Cognitive researcher Lena Petrov investigated whether environmental enrichment affects learning in laboratory rats. She housed one group of rats in standard cages and another in cages equipped with tunnels, wheels, and varied objects, then measured the number of trials each group needed to learn a maze and, separately, the percentage of the maze each group navigated correctly when retested four weeks later. Petrov argued that enrichment confers a twofold advantage: enriched rats not only acquire spatial knowledge more efficiently but also retain it more durably than rats in standard housing, a combination she contended ordinary housing cannot match.
stimulusTableHtml:
Maze Learning and Retention by Housing Condition
Housing conditionTrials to learn mazeMaze navigated correctly at 4-week retest (\%)
Standard1661
Enriched984

choices.text: The enriched group needed fewer trials to learn the maze (9 versus 16) and navigated a higher percentage correctly at retest (84% versus 61%) than the standard group.
The enriched group navigated 84% of the maze correctly at retest, while the standard group navigated 61% correctly, showing that enriched rats retained more spatial knowledge.
The enriched group needed only 9 trials to learn the maze compared with the standard group's 16 trials, demonstrating that enriched rats acquired spatial knowledge more efficiently.
The standard group needed 16 trials to learn the maze but still navigated 61% of it correctly at retest, indicating that even ordinary housing supports substantial learning and retention.

explanation: Petrov's argument is twofold: enriched rats learn more efficiently AND retain more durably. Only choice A reports both measures—fewer trials to learn (9 vs. 16) and higher retest accuracy (84% vs. 61%)—supporting the full claim. Choice B addresses only retention. Choice C addresses only acquisition efficiency. Choice D describes the standard group's performance to suggest ordinary housing suffices, contradicting Petrov's comparative claim.

targetedAbility: Select the graph description showing the cooperative outsold both other store types every quarter, supporting the analyst's conclusion of consistent advantage.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the graph that support the analyst's conclusion?
passage: A market analyst tracked average quarterly sales per location for three types of grocery retailers in a region: large chains, independent stores, and member-owned cooperatives. The analyst concluded that cooperatives consistently outperformed both other retailer types in average quarterly sales per location across all four quarters of the year studied.

choices.text: In each of the four quarters, cooperatives recorded higher average quarterly sales per location than both large chains and independent stores.
Cooperatives recorded their highest average quarterly sales per location in the fourth quarter, when they reached $420,000 per location.
In the fourth quarter, cooperatives recorded higher average quarterly sales per location than both large chains and independent stores.
Across the four quarters, large chains generally recorded lower average quarterly sales per location than independent stores did.

explanation: The conclusion is that cooperatives outperformed both other types in all four quarters. Choice A states exactly this. Choice B reports only the cooperatives' peak quarter and makes no comparison. Choice C supports the claim for only one quarter, not all four. Choice D compares chains and independents, ignoring cooperatives entirely.

targetedAbility: Select the table description showing the late-glazed pottery group had both lower porosity and higher firing temperature than the early group, supporting the archaeologist's technological-shift conclusion.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the table that support Adeyemi's argument?
passage: Archaeologist Naomi Adeyemi analyzed ceramic sherds excavated from two stratigraphic layers at a single settlement, an earlier layer and a later layer, to investigate whether potters there changed their techniques over time. For each layer she measured two properties: the average porosity of the sherds, which decreases as clay is fired more completely, and the estimated firing temperature. Adeyemi argued that the later potters had adopted a more advanced firing technology, reasoning that producing denser, less porous vessels would require sustaining higher kiln temperatures than the earlier potters had achieved—an inference that depends on both measurements moving together in the expected directions.
stimulusTableHtml:
Sherd Properties by Stratigraphic Layer
LayerAverage porosity (\%)Estimated firing temperature (° C)
Earlier19780
Later8960

choices.text: Sherds from the later layer had lower average porosity (8% versus 19%) and a higher estimated firing temperature (960°C versus 780°C) than sherds from the earlier layer.
Sherds from the later layer had lower average porosity (8% versus 19%) than sherds from the earlier layer, indicating that the later vessels were fired more completely.
Sherds from the later layer had a higher estimated firing temperature (960°C versus 780°C) than sherds from the earlier layer, indicating that later kilns reached greater heat.
Sherds from the earlier layer had higher average porosity (19% versus 8%) yet a firing temperature of 780°C, showing that even earlier potters could produce serviceable vessels.

explanation: Adeyemi's inference depends on both measurements moving as expected: lower porosity AND higher firing temperature in the later layer. Choice A reports both, supporting the full argument. Choice B reports only the porosity difference. Choice C reports only the temperature difference. Each alone leaves the inference incomplete because the argument links the two. Choice D recasts the earlier layer's data to make an unrelated point about serviceability.

targetedAbility: Select the graph description showing recycling rose only after the deposit program began, supporting the conclusion that the program rather than prior trends drove the increase.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the graph that support the researcher's conclusion?
passage: A policy researcher examined beverage-container recycling rates in a state over six years. A container-deposit program began at the start of the fourth year. The researcher concluded that the deposit program, rather than a preexisting upward trend, was responsible for the increase in recycling rates observed during the study period.

choices.text: The recycling rate held roughly steady near 40% during the first three years and then rose sharply to 68% over the three years after the deposit program began.
The recycling rate rose from 40% in the first year to 68% in the sixth year, an overall increase of 28 percentage points across the study period.
The recycling rate reached its highest value of 68% in the sixth year, the final year of the study period.
The recycling rate was higher in the three years after the deposit program began than it had been in the first year of the study.

explanation: To rule out a preexisting trend, the data must show stability before the program and increase only after. Choice A shows the rate held steady near 40% for the first three years, then rose to 68% after the program began. Choice B reports the overall increase but does not distinguish before from after, so it is consistent with a preexisting trend. Choice C reports only the peak. Choice D compares post-program years to the first year but ignores the flat pre-program period.

targetedAbility: Select the table description showing the bilingual group outperformed the monolingual group on task-switching but not on vocabulary, supporting the conclusion that the bilingual advantage is domain-specific.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the table that support Reyes's conclusion?
passage: Psycholinguist Tomas Reyes tested whether bilingualism confers a general cognitive advantage or one limited to specific abilities. He compared monolingual and bilingual adults on two measures: performance on a task-switching test, which gauges the capacity to shift flexibly between rules, and scores on a single-language vocabulary test. Reyes concluded that any bilingual advantage is domain-specific rather than general, contending that bilingual participants would surpass monolinguals on executive-control tasks such as task-switching while showing no corresponding edge—and possibly a disadvantage—on a vocabulary measure confined to one language.
stimulusTableHtml:
Mean Test Scores by Language Group
Language groupTask-switching test (score)Single-language vocabulary test (score)
Monolingual7470
Bilingual8861

choices.text: Bilingual participants scored higher than monolingual participants on the task-switching test (88 versus 74) but lower on the single-language vocabulary test (61 versus 70).
Bilingual participants scored higher than monolingual participants on the task-switching test, scoring 88 compared with the monolinguals' 74 on that measure.
Bilingual participants scored 61 on the single-language vocabulary test while monolingual participants scored 70, a difference of 9 points on that measure.
Bilingual participants outscored monolingual participants on the task-switching test and also achieved a respectable score of 61 on the single-language vocabulary test.

explanation: Reyes's domain-specific claim requires the bilingual group to exceed monolinguals on task-switching but NOT on vocabulary. Choice A reports both results in the predicted directions (higher on task-switching, lower on vocabulary), supporting the full claim. Choice B reports only the task-switching advantage. Choice C reports only the vocabulary scores. Choice D reports the task-switching advantage but mischaracterizes the lower vocabulary score as 'respectable,' obscuring the absence of an advantage that the conclusion requires.

targetedAbility: Select the graph description showing the new fertilizer produced higher yields than the standard one in every soil type tested, supporting the agronomist's conclusion.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the graph that support the agronomist's conclusion?
passage: An agronomist tested a newly developed fertilizer against a standard fertilizer by applying each to wheat plots in three soil types: sandy, loamy, and clay. After harvest, she recorded the average grain yield per plot for each combination. The agronomist concluded that the new fertilizer produced higher average yields than the standard fertilizer regardless of soil type.

choices.text: In sandy, loamy, and clay soils alike, the new fertilizer produced a higher average grain yield per plot than the standard fertilizer did.
In loamy soil, the new fertilizer produced an average grain yield per plot of 5.2 tons, the highest yield recorded for any combination in the study.
In clay soil, the new fertilizer produced a higher average grain yield per plot than the standard fertilizer did.
Across all three soil types, yields under both fertilizers were higher in loamy soil than in sandy or clay soil.

explanation: The conclusion is that the new fertilizer outyielded the standard one in every soil type. Choice A states exactly this. Choice B reports the single highest yield but makes no fertilizer-to-fertilizer comparison. Choice C supports the claim for clay soil only, not all three. Choice D compares soil types rather than fertilizers.

targetedAbility: Select the graph description showing that reported agreement is roughly flat among low-word-count debates and falls sharply only among higher-word-count debates, supporting Anand's threshold conclusion that moderator talkativeness suppresses consensus only once it becomes substantial.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the graph that support Anand's conclusion?
passage: Communication scholar Priya Anand examined recordings of structured public debates to test how a moderator's verbal behavior shapes outcomes. For each of eight debates she recorded two quantities: the number of words spoken by the moderator and the percentage of audience members who reported afterward that the debaters had reached at least partial agreement. Although a moderator's interventions are often presumed to clarify positions and thereby foster consensus, Anand concluded the opposite holds past a certain point: she argued that debates with sparing moderation differed little in reported agreement, whereas agreement dropped off steeply among the debates whose moderators spoke most, suggesting that only extended, not merely present, moderation suppresses perceived consensus.

choices.text: Among the four debates with the lowest moderator word counts, reported agreement was relatively similar, but among the four debates with higher word counts, reported agreement was progressively and substantially lower as word count rose.

Across all eight debates, reported agreement tended to decline as the moderator's word count increased, with each successive debate showing a somewhat lower agreement percentage than the debate just below it in word count.

The debate with the highest moderator word count had one of the lowest reported-agreement percentages, whereas the debate with the lowest word count had the highest reported-agreement percentage of any debate that Anand studied.

Among the four debates with the lowest moderator word counts, reported agreement was relatively low, but among the four debates with higher word counts, reported agreement tended to rise somewhat as the moderator's word count increased.

explanation: Anand's specific conclusion is a threshold claim: sparing moderation barely affects agreement, but agreement falls steeply once moderation becomes extensive. Choice A captures both halves, a roughly flat low-word-count region and a steep decline at high word counts, which is exactly the pattern the threshold argument requires. Choice B describes a single steady, uniform decline across all eight debates; this is a true-looking trend reading but contradicts the threshold structure (flat-then-steep) that Anand actually argued for, so it fits the data poorly and supports the wrong claim. Choice C cites the two endpoint debates, which are consistent with Anand's view but describe only the extremes and say nothing about the flat-then-falling shape that distinguishes her threshold conclusion from an ordinary inverse trend. Choice D reverses the high-word-count relationship, showing agreement rising rather than falling among the talkative-moderator debates.

targetedAbility: Select the table description showing the redesigned trail had both fewer reported injuries and more annual visitors than the old trail, supporting the conclusion that the redesign improved safety without reducing use.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the table that support the department's conclusion?
passage: A parks department redesigned a popular hiking trail, widening switchbacks and adding railings, and then compared safety and usage data for the year before and the year after the redesign. The department concluded that the redesign improved safety without discouraging visitors, noting that the trail saw both fewer reported injuries and more annual visitors after the changes.
stimulusTableHtml:
Trail Safety and Usage Before and After Redesign
PeriodReported injuriesAnnual visitors
Year before redesign2318,400
Year after redesign922,700

choices.text: After the redesign, reported injuries fell from 23 to 9 while annual visitors rose from 18,400 to 22,700.
After the redesign, reported injuries fell from 23 to 9, indicating that the trail became safer for hikers.
After the redesign, annual visitors rose from 18,400 to 22,700, indicating that more people used the trail.
Before the redesign, the trail recorded 23 reported injuries among 18,400 annual visitors, a relatively low injury rate.

explanation: The conclusion has two parts: improved safety AND no loss of visitors. Choice A reports both—injuries fell from 23 to 9 and visitors rose from 18,400 to 22,700. Choice B addresses only safety. Choice C addresses only usage. Choice D describes the pre-redesign year to make a point about injury rate, which does not support the conclusion about the redesign's effects.

targetedAbility: Select the bar-graph description showing that the deep-litter coop reduced ammonia at both 4 and 8 weeks relative to the control, supporting the conclusion that the bedding lowers ammonia over time.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the graph that support Verhoeven and colleagues' conclusion?
passage: Ammonia accumulation in poultry housing harms birds' respiratory health and reduces growth, so producers seek inexpensive ways to limit it. Agronomist Dolores Verhoeven and colleagues compared a conventional sawdust floor with a "deep-litter" system, in which a thick layer of carbon-rich bedding is left to compost in place and is periodically turned. Measuring air ammonia concentration in identical coops at four weeks and again at eight weeks, the team argued that, although deep litter requires more initial material, it suppresses ammonia more effectively as a flock matures and waste accumulates, when conventional bedding tends to perform worst.

choices.text: In the conventional system, ammonia concentration was higher at four weeks than at eight weeks, whereas in the deep-litter system, ammonia concentration rose between the two measurements.

At both four and eight weeks, the deep-litter system showed a lower ammonia concentration than the conventional system, and the gap between the two systems was larger at eight weeks than at four weeks.
At four weeks, the deep-litter system had a lower ammonia concentration than the conventional system, but by eight weeks both systems had reached an ammonia concentration above 20 parts per million.
In both systems, ammonia concentration increased from four weeks to eight weeks, indicating that neither bedding type prevented ammonia from accumulating as the flock matured.

explanation: The conclusion is that deep litter suppresses ammonia more effectively as the flock matures and waste accumulates. The graph shows deep litter below conventional at both four weeks (15 vs. 22 ppm) and eight weeks (18 vs. 31 ppm), with the gap widening from 7 ppm to 13 ppm; choice B captures both the consistent advantage and its growth over time, directly supporting the conclusion. Choice A reverses the conventional trend (conventional rises, not falls) and misstates deep litter. Choice C cites a true detail about the eight-week values but does not address the comparison across time that the conclusion depends on. Choice D is true that both rise but ignores that deep litter stays lower, so it fails to support the claim that deep litter performs better as waste accumulates.

targetedAbility: Choose the graph description showing that the restored wetland's water clarity rose while the control wetland's stayed roughly flat, supporting the student's conclusion that restoration improved clarity.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the graph that support the student's conclusion?
passage: A student is studying how wetland restoration affects water clarity, measured as the depth in centimeters at which a standard disk remains visible. Comparing a wetland where native plants were reintroduced with an untreated control wetland nearby, the student concludes that restoration improved water clarity over the three-year study, whereas the control wetland's clarity changed little.

choices.text: In the restored wetland, the visibility depth increased from Year 1 to Year 3, while in the control wetland the visibility depth stayed close to its Year 1 value.
In both the restored wetland and the control wetland, the visibility depth increased steadily from Year 1 to Year 3.
In the restored wetland, the visibility depth was greater than in the control wetland in every year of the study.
In the control wetland, the visibility depth decreased from Year 1 to Year 3, while in the restored wetland it remained nearly constant.

explanation: The conclusion has two parts: clarity improved in the restored wetland and changed little in the control. The graph shows restored visibility climbing from 40 to 75 cm while the control hovers near 38-41 cm; choice A states both parts and supports the conclusion. Choice B is wrong because the control did not increase steadily. Choice C reports a true cross-wetland difference but does not address change over time, which the conclusion concerns. Choice D reverses the relationship, claiming the control fell and the restored stayed flat.

targetedAbility: Select the table reading showing the higher-spacing planting produced more grain per plant yet the closer spacing produced more grain per hectare, supporting the conclusion that density trades individual yield for area yield.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the table that support Ek's conclusion?
passage: Crop scientists studying sorghum routinely distinguish yield per plant from yield per unit of land, since the two measures need not move in the same direction as cultivation conditions change. Investigating how planting density affects output, researcher Tomas Ek grew sorghum at three spacings and recorded both the average grain mass produced by an individual plant and the total grain harvested per hectare. Although he acknowledged that wider spacing eases competition for water and nutrients so that each plant yields more, Ek concluded that the densest planting nonetheless produces the greatest harvest per hectare, because the larger number of plants more than compensates for each plant's smaller individual output.
stimulusTableHtml:
Sorghum Yield by Planting Spacing
Spacing (cm)Grain per plant (g)Grain per hectare (t)
10387.9
20546.6
30715.2

choices.text: As spacing widened from 10 cm to 30 cm, grain per plant climbed steadily from 38 g to 71 g, yet grain per hectare declined from 7.9 t to 5.2 t, so the closest spacing paired the lowest per-plant yield with the highest per-hectare yield.

As spacing widened from 10 cm to 30 cm, grain per plant climbed steadily from 38 g to 71 g, so the widest spacing both maximized the output of each individual plant and delivered the largest total harvest measured per hectare.
At the intermediate 20 cm spacing, grain per plant reached 54 g and grain per hectare reached 6.6 t, values that each fell between the corresponding figures recorded at the 10 cm and 30 cm spacings.

As spacing widened from 10 cm to 30 cm, grain per hectare climbed from 5.2 t to 7.9 t while grain per plant declined, so the widest spacing paired the highest per-hectare yield with the lowest per-plant yield.

explanation: Ek's two-part conclusion is that wider spacing raises per-plant yield but the densest planting maximizes per-hectare yield. The table shows per-plant grain rising with spacing (38, 54, 71 g) while per-hectare grain falls (7.9, 6.6, 5.2 t), so the 10 cm spacing has the lowest per-plant but highest per-hectare yield; choice A states exactly this trade-off. Choice B correctly reports the rising per-plant figures but then claims the widest spacing also gave the largest per-hectare harvest, which inverts the per-hectare column and erases the trade-off. Choice C reports two accurate intermediate values but addresses only the middle spacing, never establishing the opposing endpoints the conclusion depends on. Choice D pairs accurate-sounding endpoint numbers with the wrong columns, attaching the highest per-hectare yield to the widest spacing.

targetedAbility: Choose the graph description showing that attendance at both museum branches rose after the free-admission policy, supporting the student's conclusion of a shared upward shift.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the graph that support the student's conclusion?
passage: A student is examining monthly visitor counts at two branches of a regional art museum before and after the museum eliminated admission fees in July. Comparing the downtown branch and the riverside branch, the student concludes that both branches saw higher attendance in the three months after the policy change than in the three months before it.

choices.text: At both the downtown and riverside branches, average monthly attendance was higher in the three months after July than in the three months before July.
At the downtown branch, average monthly attendance was higher after July, but at the riverside branch it was about the same before and after July.
The downtown branch recorded higher attendance than the riverside branch in every month shown on the graph.
At both branches, average monthly attendance reached its highest value in the month of July itself.

explanation: The conclusion is that both branches had higher attendance after the policy than before. The graph shows downtown rising from roughly 3,000 to 4,500 and riverside from roughly 1,800 to 2,700; choice A reports the post-policy increase at both branches and supports the conclusion. Choice B denies the increase at riverside, contradicting the data. Choice C reports a true cross-branch comparison but says nothing about the before/after change. Choice D misreads the timing, since the peaks occur in months after July, not in July.

targetedAbility: Select the table reading that supports the conclusion that higher niobium content tracks with both a higher melting point and a lower density, requiring synthesis of both property columns across the alloy that anchors the relationship.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the table that support Anand's conclusion?
passage: Aerospace engineers prize alloys that pair a high melting point with low density, since such materials must endure engine heat without adding weight that would penalize fuel efficiency. Metallurgist Priya Anand prepared four experimental titanium alloys that differed only in their niobium content and measured the melting point and the density of each. Although she cautioned that other characteristics, such as fatigue resistance, would still require separate testing before any formulation could be adopted, Anand argued from these initial measurements that raising the proportion of niobium in the alloy is associated with both a higher melting point and a lower density, a combination that would make the higher-niobium formulations particularly promising for the thermal and structural demands placed on jet-engine components.
stimulusTableHtml:
Properties of Four Titanium-Niobium Alloys
AlloyNiobium content (\%)Melting point (° C)Density (g/cm3)
Alloy 141{,}6404.71
Alloy 281{,}6654.62
Alloy 3121{,}6904.53
Alloy 4161{,}7104.42

choices.text: Alloy 4, which contained the most niobium at 16%, recorded the highest melting point at 1,710°C and the lowest density at 4.42 g/cm³ of the four alloys Anand tested.
Alloy 3, which contained 12% niobium, recorded a melting point of 1,690°C and a density of 4.53 g/cm³, both intermediate among the four alloys that Anand prepared for her study.
Alloy 4, which contained the most niobium at 16%, recorded the highest melting point at 1,710°C, and its density at 4.42 g/cm³ ranked second-lowest among the four alloys.
Alloy 1, which contained the least niobium at 4%, recorded the lowest melting point at 1,640°C and a density of 4.71 g/cm³, the highest density of the four alloys Anand tested.

explanation: Anand's conclusion links higher niobium content to both a higher melting point and a lower density. The table shows niobium rising 4, 8, 12, 16% as melting point rises 1,640, 1,665, 1,690, 1,710°C and density falls 4.71, 4.62, 4.53, 4.42 g/cm³; the highest-niobium Alloy 4 therefore has the highest melting point and the lowest density, exactly as choice A states, capturing both halves of the claim at the endpoint that anchors the relationship. Choice B reports a single alloy's accurate intermediate values for both properties but says nothing about how those values relate to niobium content across the set, so it cannot establish either association. Choice C gives Alloy 4 the correct highest melting point but ranks its density only second-lowest, misreading the density column where 4.42 is in fact the lowest, undercutting the lower-density half of the claim. Choice D accurately reports Alloy 1's lowest niobium, lowest melting point, and highest density, but it documents the low-niobium endpoint in isolation rather than showing the dual association the conclusion draws.

targetedAbility: Select the data reading that completes a text about annotation, showing the annotating group read more slowly yet recalled more, distinguishing the full speed-for-retention trade-off from partially accurate near-misses.
prompt: Which choice most effectively uses data from the graph to complete the text?
passage: Educational psychologist Lin Hara wondered whether the common practice of annotating a text while reading—underlining passages and writing margin notes—affects both reading speed and later recall, two outcomes that might not respond to annotation in the same way. She assigned readers either to annotate a history passage or to read it without marking it, then measured each group's average reading rate in words per minute as well as the percentage of key facts each group correctly recalled a full day later. Her findings suggest that annotation imposes a measurable cost in reading speed while yielding a benefit in retention, illustrating a trade-off that readers face when deciding whether the slower pace of marking up a text is ultimately worthwhile: ______

choices.text: the annotating group read at a slower average rate than the non-annotating group but recalled a higher percentage of the passage's key facts a full day later.
the annotating group read at a slower average rate than the non-annotating group, yet the two groups recalled a closely comparable percentage of the passage's key facts a day later.
the annotating group recalled a higher percentage of the passage's key facts than the non-annotating group did, having read the passage at a faster average rate as well.
the non-annotating group read at a faster average rate than the annotating group did and also recalled a modestly higher percentage of the passage's key facts a day later.

explanation: The text frames a trade-off in which annotation costs reading speed but benefits retention. The graph shows the annotating group reading more slowly (140 vs. 205 wpm) yet recalling more (78% vs. 61%); choice A states both halves of that trade-off and so completes the sentence the text sets up. Choice B correctly reports the slower reading rate for the annotators but then claims the two groups recalled a comparable percentage, erasing the 78%-versus-61% retention benefit that the trade-off depends on. Choice C correctly credits the annotators with higher recall but pairs that with a faster reading rate, which both contradicts the data and removes the speed cost the trade-off requires. Choice D accurately reports the faster rate for the non-annotators but assigns them the higher recall as well, reversing the recall result and yielding no trade-off at all.

targetedAbility: Select the graph description that supports the conclusion that canopy shade buffered the shaded colony against heat, distinguishing the full magnitude contrast from a partial reading that omits how much more the exposed colony lost.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the graph that support Quint's conclusion?
passage: Bats that roost in tree cavities may be vulnerable to extreme heat, since prolonged high temperatures can be lethal to tightly clustered animals that cannot easily disperse. Ecologist Marisol Quint tracked two nearby colonies of a cave-myotis species through a severe summer heat wave: one roosting in cavities shaded by dense canopy and one in cavities exposed to direct afternoon sun. Recording each colony's size weekly across the four weeks of the heat wave, Quint concluded that, although both colonies lost individuals as temperatures climbed, the canopy shade substantially buffered the shaded colony, which retained a far greater share of its members through the heat wave's end than the more exposed colony was able to retain.

choices.text: Over the four weeks, the shaded colony slipped from 320 to 290 individuals, a loss near 9%, while the exposed colony fell from 310 to 150 individuals, a loss of roughly 52%.
Over the four weeks, the shaded colony declined from 320 to 290 individuals and the exposed colony declined from 310 to 150 individuals, so each colony recorded fewer members by the heat wave's end.
Over the four weeks, the shaded colony's losses grew larger each week, with its weekly drop widening from 8 individuals between weeks one and two to 10 between weeks three and four.
Over the four weeks, both colonies began near 315 individuals and then lost members at a similar pace through the first two weeks before their weekly counts began to diverge.

explanation: Quint's conclusion is that both colonies lost members but shade buffered the shaded colony, which retained a far greater share of its members. The graph shows the shaded colony falling only from 320 to 290 (about 9%) while the exposed colony fell from 310 to 150 (about 52%); choice A quantifies both declines and the stark gap between them, supplying exactly the contrast the conclusion rests on. Choice B accurately reports both ending counts and notes both colonies declined, but it presents the two losses side by side without conveying the disproportion, so it documents that both fell rather than that the shaded colony retained far more. Choice C focuses on the shaded colony's modest week-to-week pattern in isolation, a true detail that says nothing about how the two colonies compare. Choice D emphasizes the early period of similar decline, a true but pre-divergence observation that misses the differential survival the conclusion highlights.

targetedAbility: Select the data reading that completes a text about glaze firing, showing the higher firing temperature produced both greater density and fewer pinholes, distinguishing the full dual improvement from partially accurate near-misses.
prompt: Which choice most effectively uses data from the graph to complete the text?
passage: Ceramicists know that the temperature at which a glaze is fired shapes the finished surface, but the precise effects can be difficult to predict without controlled testing across a range of conditions. Studio researcher Hana Oduya fired identical glazed tiles in two kilns, one reaching a peak of 1,180°C and the other a peak of 1,250°C, and then measured two qualities of the cooled glaze: its density, in grams per cubic centimeter, and the average number of surface pinholes per tile, a defect that mars the finished appearance. Her data indicate that firing at the higher peak temperature improves the glaze in both of the respects she examined, even though the two qualities might in principle have responded to the change in opposite directions: ______

choices.text: tiles fired at 1,250°C averaged both a higher glaze density and fewer surface pinholes per tile than tiles fired at 1,180°C did.
tiles fired at 1,250°C averaged a higher glaze density than tiles fired at 1,180°C, while the two groups averaged a roughly equal number of surface pinholes per tile.
tiles fired at 1,250°C averaged fewer surface pinholes per tile than tiles fired at 1,180°C, while the two groups reached a roughly equal glaze density.
tiles fired at 1,180°C averaged both a higher glaze density and more surface pinholes per tile than tiles fired at 1,250°C did.

explanation: The text asserts that the higher firing temperature improves the glaze in both respects: greater density and fewer pinholes. The graph shows the 1,250°C tiles with higher density (2.62 vs. 2.41 g/cm³) and fewer pinholes (3 vs. 9); choice A reports both improvements and so completes the sentence as the text demands. Choice B correctly credits the hotter firing with higher density but claims the pinhole counts were roughly equal, omitting the clear reduction from 9 to 3 and supporting only one of the two improvements. Choice C correctly credits the hotter firing with fewer pinholes but claims the densities were roughly equal, omitting the rise from 2.41 to 2.62 and again confirming only one improvement. Choice D reports both directions accurately for the variables but attributes both the higher density and the higher pinhole count to the lower-temperature tiles, mixing a reversed density with the correct pinhole comparison so that neither improvement is correctly assigned to the hotter firing.

targetedAbility: Select the table reading that supports the conclusion that fewer-speaker dialects yielded more transcribed folktales, distinguishing the full inverse relationship across the extremes from partial and reversed near-misses.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the table that support Bankole's conclusion?
passage: Linguists documenting endangered languages sometimes find that the vitality of a recorded oral tradition does not simply mirror the number of people who still speak a language. Surveying four regional dialects of a coastal language, folklorist Ade Bankole recorded both the estimated number of fluent speakers of each dialect and the number of distinct folktales that field researchers had transcribed from each. Noting that the most widely spoken dialect had largely been studied for commercial rather than literary purposes, Bankole concluded that, within this particular sample, the dialects with fewer fluent speakers had actually yielded more transcribed folktales than the dialects spoken by greater numbers of people had yielded.
stimulusTableHtml:
Fluent Speakers and Transcribed Folktales by Dialect
DialectFluent speakersTranscribed folktales
Coastal9{,}20014
Valley5{,}40031
Forest2{,}80049
Highland1{,}10063

choices.text: The Coastal dialect, with the most fluent speakers at 9,200, had the fewest transcribed folktales at 14, while the Highland dialect, with the fewest speakers at 1,100, had the most folktales at 63.

The Forest dialect, with 2,800 fluent speakers, had 49 transcribed folktales, while the Valley dialect, with 5,400 speakers, had 31 folktales, so the dialect with more speakers had fewer folktales here.

The Coastal dialect, with the most fluent speakers at 9,200, also had the most transcribed folktales at 63, while the Highland dialect, with the fewest speakers at 1,100, had the fewest folktales at 14.
Across the four dialects the number of fluent speakers fell steadily from 9,200 to 1,100, with each dialect counting fewer speakers than the dialect listed in the row above it.

explanation: Bankole's conclusion is that, in this sample, dialects with fewer speakers yielded more transcribed folktales. The table shows speakers descending (9,200, 5,400, 2,800, 1,100) as folktales ascend (14, 31, 49, 63), so the most-spoken Coastal dialect has the fewest folktales and the least-spoken Highland dialect the most; choice A captures the full inverse relationship across the extremes that span the sample. Choice B reports two accurate, mutually consistent middle rows that do illustrate the inverse direction, but it draws only on the two intermediate dialects and never invokes the extremes, so it offers a weaker, narrower slice of the relationship than the conclusion's claim about the whole sample requires. Choice C pairs the correct speaker totals with swapped folktale totals, attaching the largest folktale count to the most-spoken dialect and so reversing the relationship. Choice D accurately describes the steady decline in the speaker column alone but omits the folktale counts entirely, addressing only one of the two variables the conclusion links.

targetedAbility: Select the graph description that supports the conclusion that buffer strips reduced nitrate, distinguishing the full treated-decline-versus-stable-control contrast from a near-miss that overstates the control's change.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the graph that support Saaristo's conclusion?
passage: Vegetated buffer strips planted along waterways are thought to intercept agricultural runoff before it reaches the water, lowering the nitrate concentrations that can fuel harmful algal blooms downstream. Hydrologist Erik Saaristo monitored a stream reach where a buffer strip was planted in Year 2, alongside an unbuffered tributary that drained comparable farmland under similar conditions. Tracking nitrate concentration in milligrams per liter at both sites from Year 1 through Year 4, Saaristo concluded that the buffer strip reduced nitrate in the treated reach, reasoning that its concentration dropped markedly after planting while the untreated tributary, exposed to similar farming throughout, showed no comparable decline over the same four-year period.

choices.text: In the buffered reach, nitrate fell from 8.1 mg/L in Year 1 to 3.4 mg/L in Year 4, while in the unbuffered tributary it remained between 7.6 and 8.0 mg/L across all four years.
In the buffered reach, nitrate fell from 8.1 mg/L in Year 1 to 3.4 mg/L in Year 4, while in the unbuffered tributary it edged down from 7.9 to 7.6 mg/L over the same four years.
In the buffered reach, nitrate held near 8 mg/L through Year 2 and then declined, reaching 5.2 mg/L in Year 3 and 3.4 mg/L in Year 4 after the buffer strip was planted.
In Year 1, before the buffer strip was planted, the buffered reach and the unbuffered tributary recorded nearly identical nitrate concentrations, each measuring about 8 mg/L at the study's outset.

explanation: Saaristo's conclusion is that the buffer reduced nitrate, evidenced by a marked drop in the treated reach after planting while the untreated tributary showed no comparable decline. The graph shows the buffered reach falling from 8.1 to 3.4 mg/L while the tributary stays between 7.6 and 8.0; choice A reports both the sharp treated decline and the essentially flat control, supplying the full contrast the conclusion requires. Choice B accurately reports the buffered reach's drop but characterizes the tributary as edging down from 7.9 to 7.6, treating a trivial fluctuation within a flat series as a genuine decline and thereby weakening the no-comparable-decline contrast the conclusion depends on. Choice C correctly traces the buffered reach's post-planting decline but describes only that one site, never invoking the untreated tributary needed to show the effect was specific to the buffer. Choice D accurately notes the similar Year 1 baseline but focuses on the pre-planting starting point rather than the post-planting change that supports the buffer's effect.

targetedAbility: Select the line-graph reading showing that the native shrub's seedling survival exceeded the invasive shrub's only in deep shade, supporting the shade-tolerance conclusion while rejecting full-sun and intermediate readings.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the graph that support Okafor's conclusion?
passage: In many temperate forests, an invasive shrub spreads rapidly in disturbed, well-lit gaps yet rarely dominates the dim understory beneath an intact canopy. Botanist Lena Okafor tracked the percentage of seedlings of a native shrub and the invasive shrub that survived their first year across a gradient of light availability, expressed as the percentage of full sunlight reaching the forest floor. Although the invasive shrub outperformed the native at higher light levels, Okafor concluded that the native shrub's superior tolerance of deep shade, rather than any advantage in brighter conditions, explains why the native persists where the canopy remains closed.

choices.text: At 10% of full sunlight, a greater percentage of native seedlings survived than invasive seedlings, but at 75% of full sunlight, a greater percentage of invasive seedlings survived than native seedlings.
At every light level measured, a greater percentage of native seedlings survived than invasive seedlings, with the largest difference occurring at 10% of full sunlight.
At 10% of full sunlight, more than half of the native seedlings survived, and survival of both shrubs increased steadily as light availability rose toward 75% of full sunlight.
At 75% of full sunlight, a greater percentage of invasive seedlings survived than native seedlings, and this gap was wider than the gap between the two shrubs at 10% of full sunlight.

explanation: Okafor concluded that the native shrub's advantage lies specifically in deep shade, not in brighter conditions. Choice A is correct because it shows the native outperforming the invasive at the lowest light level (10% of full sunlight: native 68%, invasive 22%) while the invasive outperforms the native at high light (75%: native 30%, invasive 74%); this pattern pinpoints the native's edge as a deep-shade phenomenon. Choice B contradicts the figure, which shows the invasive surviving at higher rates than the native at 50% and 75% sunlight, so the native does not lead at every light level. Choice C misreads the trend: native survival declines as light rises rather than increasing steadily for both shrubs. Choice D both shifts focus to where the invasive wins and misstates the figure, since the gap at 75% sunlight (44 percentage points) is narrower, not wider, than the gap at 10% sunlight (46 percentage points).

targetedAbility: Select the table reading showing that the two-step glaze reduced cracking across both clay types while raising gloss, supporting the conclusion that its advantage held regardless of clay body, where distractors misattribute the gloss gain to only one clay or reverse the cross-clay pattern.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the table that support Velasquez's conclusion?
passage: Archaeologists studying an ancient ceramic workshop have long debated whether its unusual two-step glazing method, in which a vessel was fired, coated a second time, and refired, offered any real advantage over the conventional single-step method, particularly because the workshop drew on two distinct local clays whose firing behavior differed considerably. Materials scientist Dario Velasquez reconstructed both methods, producing batches of identical bowls from each clay, and recorded for every batch the average number of surface cracks per bowl and the average surface gloss on a standardized scale. Although the additional firing demanded extra labor, Velasquez argued that the two-step method would have been worthwhile because its benefits to both durability and appearance did not depend on which of the two clays a potter happened to use.
stimulusTableHtml:
Reconstructed Glazing Methods: Bowl Measurements by Clay Type
Clay typeGlazing methodAvg. surface cracks per bowlAvg. surface gloss (0--100 scale)
Iron-richSingle-step5.158
Iron-richTwo-step2.079
CalcareousSingle-step3.866
CalcareousTwo-step1.688

choices.text: For each of the two clays, bowls made with the two-step method averaged fewer surface cracks and higher surface gloss than bowls of the same clay made with the single-step method.

For each of the two clays, the two-step method reduced the average number of surface cracks per bowl, and for the calcareous clay it also produced the single highest surface gloss recorded among all four batches.

For each of the two clays, bowls made with the two-step method averaged higher surface gloss than their single-step counterparts, and the iron-rich clay showed the larger reduction in average surface cracks under the two-step method.

For the iron-rich clay, the two-step method averaged fewer surface cracks and higher gloss than the single-step method, whereas for the calcareous clay the single-step method averaged the lower number of surface cracks.

explanation: Velasquez's conclusion is that the two-step method improved both durability (fewer cracks) and appearance (higher gloss) and that this advantage held for both clays, independent of which clay was used. Choice A is correct because it states that for each clay the two-step method yielded both fewer cracks and higher gloss, which is exactly the cross-clay, two-attribute pattern the table shows (iron-rich: 5.1 to 2.0 cracks, 58 to 79 gloss; calcareous: 3.8 to 1.6 cracks, 66 to 88 gloss). Choice B is true on every point it asserts but supports only the durability claim for both clays while restricting the gloss evidence to a single clay, so it does not establish that the appearance benefit was clay-independent. Choice C is likewise true in each statement yet shifts the focus to which clay improved more, addressing the magnitude of the crack reduction rather than the conclusion that both benefits appeared for both clays. Choice D reverses the crack relationship for the calcareous clay, claiming the single-step method cracked less, which contradicts the table.

targetedAbility: Recognize that a comparably large reduction in soil nitrogen loss in the untreated control plots undermines the claim that the biochar amendment alone was responsible for the observed reduction.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the graph that weaken Okonkwo's conclusion?
passage: Agronomist Dolores Okonkwo investigated whether adding biochar (a charcoal-like soil amendment) reduces the loss of nitrogen from cropland into nearby waterways, a process that degrades water quality. Over a single growing season, she measured cumulative nitrogen runoff from four experimental plots that received biochar and from four untreated control plots, all planted with the same maize variety and exposed to identical rainfall. Observing that runoff from the biochar plots fell sharply between the season's start and end, Okonkwo concluded that the biochar amendment was responsible for the reduction in nitrogen lost from the treated plots.

choices.text: Cumulative nitrogen runoff from the biochar plots was lower at the end of the season than cumulative runoff from the control plots was at the end of the season.
Cumulative nitrogen runoff from the control plots also declined substantially between the start and the end of the season, ending well below its starting level.
At the start of the season, cumulative nitrogen runoff from the biochar plots was nearly equal to cumulative runoff from the control plots.
The steepest single-month decline in cumulative nitrogen runoff from the biochar plots occurred during the first month rather than the final month of the season.

explanation: Okonkwo concluded that the biochar amendment caused the reduction in nitrogen runoff in the treated plots. If the untreated control plots, which received no biochar, showed a comparably substantial decline over the same season, then some factor other than biochar must account for the reduction, which weakens her conclusion. Choice B states exactly this: runoff from the control plots also declined substantially (from 45 to 26 kg per hectare), ending well below its starting level. Choice A is true but does not weaken the conclusion; lower end-of-season runoff in the biochar plots than in the control plots is consistent with biochar being effective. Choice C describes the starting values being nearly equal, which establishes a fair baseline rather than undercutting the causal claim. Choice D concerns the timing of the decline within the biochar plots and says nothing about whether biochar, rather than another factor, caused it.

targetedAbility: Identify that a similar increase in recall scores among participants who did not nap undermines the claim that the afternoon nap caused the memory improvement.
prompt: Which choice best describes data from the graph that weaken the researcher's conclusion?
passage: A psychology researcher tested whether a brief afternoon nap improves memory. In the morning, two groups of volunteers each studied the same list of forty word pairs and took an initial recall test. After lunch, one group took a 30-minute nap while the other group stayed awake and rested quietly. Both groups then took a second recall test. The researcher found that the nap group's average recall score rose from the first test to the second and concluded that the afternoon nap was responsible for the improvement in the nap group's memory performance.

choices.text: The nap group's average recall score on the first test was slightly higher than the no-nap group's average recall score on the first test.
The no-nap group's average recall score also rose by a similar amount from the first test to the second test.
The nap group's average recall score on the second test was higher than the no-nap group's average recall score on the second test.
Both groups answered fewer than thirty of the forty word pairs correctly on the first recall test.

explanation: The researcher claimed the nap caused the nap group's improvement. If the no-nap group, which did not nap, improved by a similar amount from the first test to the second (a rise of 6 points, comparable to the nap group's rise of 7 points), then the improvement cannot be attributed specifically to the nap, which weakens the conclusion. Choice B states this directly. Choice A reports a small starting difference favoring the nap group but says nothing about whether the nap caused the gain. Choice C shows the nap group scoring higher on the second test, which is consistent with the nap helping and does not weaken the claim. Choice D describes both groups' raw scores on the first test without comparing changes, so it does not bear on the cause of the improvement.

targetedAbility: Retrieve the average annual rainfall of the Atacama Desert directly from the table without confusing it with another desert's value.
prompt: According to the table, what is the average annual rainfall of the Atacama Desert?
passage: Geographer Lucia Marin compared the average annual rainfall of four major deserts to illustrate how dramatically dry regions can differ from one another, with some receiving virtually no precipitation while others record measurable amounts each year.
stimulusTableHtml:
Average Annual Rainfall of Four Deserts
DesertAverage annual rainfall (millimeters)
Sahara15
Atacama1
Gobi94
Mojave212

choices.text: 15 millimeters
1 millimeter
212 millimeters
94 millimeters

explanation: The table lists the Atacama Desert's average annual rainfall as 1 millimeter, so (B) is correct. (A) is the value for the Sahara Desert, (C) is the value for the Mojave Desert, and (D) is the value for the Gobi Desert, so each names a different desert's rainfall rather than the Atacama's.

targetedAbility: Read the number of paintings completed by Berthe Morisot from a bar graph without misreading an adjacent artist's bar.
prompt: According to the graph, how many paintings did Berthe Morisot complete?
passage: Art historian Tomas Okafor catalogued the surviving paintings of four nineteenth-century Impressionist artists. His count revealed that the artists varied considerably in how many finished works each left behind in major museum collections.
explanation: The bar for Berthe Morisot reaches 275, so (C) is correct. (A) is the value for Claude Monet, (B) is the value for Camille Pissarro, and (D) is the value for Mary Cassatt, so each gives a different artist's total rather than Morisot's.

targetedAbility: Retrieve the boiling point of ethanol from a table without confusing it with another liquid's boiling point.
prompt: According to the table, what is the boiling point of ethanol?
passage: In a chemistry demonstration, instructor Priya Nair measured the boiling points of four common laboratory liquids at standard pressure. Her measurements showed that each liquid boils at a distinctly different temperature under the same conditions.
stimulusTableHtml:
Boiling Points of Four Laboratory Liquids
LiquidBoiling point (° C)
Water100
Ethanol78
Acetone56
Acetic acid118
explanation: The table lists the boiling point of ethanol as 78 °C, so (B) is correct. (A) is the boiling point of water, (C) is the boiling point of acetone, and (D) is the boiling point of acetic acid, so each names a different liquid's value rather than ethanol's.

targetedAbility: Read the population of the city of Tarnaby in 1900 from a line graph without confusing it with another year's value.
prompt: According to the graph, what was the population of Tarnaby in 1900?
passage: Historian Greta Lindqvist traced the population of the small mining town of Tarnaby across the twentieth century. Her data revealed that the town grew steadily in some decades and declined in others as the local mines opened and closed.
explanation: The line graph shows the population at 1,200 in the year 1900, so (A) is correct. (B) is the population in 1925, (C) is the population in 1950, and (D) is the population in 1975, so each names a different year's value rather than the 1900 figure.

targetedAbility: Retrieve the number of moons of Neptune from a table without confusing it with another planet's count.
prompt: According to the table, how many confirmed moons does Neptune have?
passage: Astronomer Hassan Reyes prepared a reference table listing the number of confirmed moons orbiting four planets in our solar system. His table showed that the planets differ widely in how many natural satellites each one has.
stimulusTableHtml:

Jupiter95

explanation: The table lists Neptune as having 16 confirmed moons, so (B) is correct. (A) is the count for Jupiter, (C) is the count for Saturn, and (D) is the count for Uranus, so each names a different planet's total rather than Neptune's.

targetedAbility: Read the average wingspan of the Andean condor from a bar graph without misreading another bird's bar.
prompt: According to the graph, what is the average wingspan of the Andean condor?
passage: Ornithologist Dakarai Mwangi measured the average wingspan of four large flying birds for a field guide. His measurements demonstrated that wingspan varies substantially even among birds that all soar at high altitudes.

choices.text: 320 centimeters
230 centimeters
180 centimeters

explanation: The bar for the Andean condor reaches 320 centimeters, so (A) is correct. (B) is the wingspan of the golden eagle, (C) is the wingspan of the great horned owl, and (D) is the wingspan of the wandering albatross, so each gives a different bird's value rather than the condor's.

targetedAbility: Retrieve the year a particular novel was published from a table without confusing it with another novel's year.
prompt: According to the table, in what year was the novel The Glass Orchard published?
passage: Literature student Amara Boateng built a timeline of four debut novels by emerging authors. Her timeline recorded the year each novel first appeared in print, showing that the books were published across more than a decade.
stimulusTableHtml:

explanation: The table lists The Glass Orchard as published in 2011, so (C) is correct. (A) is the year for Northern Tides, (B) is the year for Saltmarsh, and (D) is the year for The Copper Field, so each names a different novel's publication year rather than that of The Glass Orchard.

targetedAbility: Read the average daily attendance at one museum exhibit from a bar graph without misreading an adjacent exhibit's bar.
prompt: According to the graph, what was the average daily attendance at the Ancient Egypt exhibit?
passage: Museum analyst Felix Romano tracked the average daily attendance at four special exhibits during one summer. His report showed that some exhibits drew far larger crowds than others over the course of the season.
explanation: The bar for the Ancient Egypt exhibit reaches 910, so (B) is correct. (A) is the attendance at the Modern Sculpture exhibit, (C) is the attendance at the Dinosaurs exhibit, and (D) is the attendance at the Photography exhibit, so each gives a different exhibit's value rather than Ancient Egypt's.

targetedAbility: Retrieve the length of one river from a table without confusing it with another river's length.
prompt: According to the table, what is the length of the Danube River?
passage: For a geography lesson, teacher Ingrid Solberg compiled the lengths of four major rivers on different continents. Her table revealed that the rivers differ greatly in length, with some stretching thousands of kilometers farther than others.

targetedAbility: Read the percentage of households owning a radio in one survey year from a line graph without confusing it with another year's value.
prompt: According to the graph, what percentage of households owned a radio in 1930?
passage: Sociologist Mei Tanaka studied how radio ownership spread among households in one region during the early twentieth century. Her survey recorded the percentage of households that owned at least one radio at four points in time.
explanation: The line graph shows that 46% of households owned a radio in 1930, so (B) is correct. (A) is the percentage in 1920, (C) is the percentage in 1940, and (D) is the percentage in 1950, so each names a different survey year's value rather than the 1930 figure.

targetedAbility: Identify the experimental finding that mycorrhizal fungi reroute carbon toward shaded seedlings, supporting the claim that the network responds to recipient need rather than donor proximity.
prompt: Which finding, if true, would most directly support Vasudevan's proposal?
passage: Many forest trees are linked belowground by mycorrhizal fungi, which form networks through which carbon compounds can move between individual plants. Ecologist Priya Vasudevan has proposed that these networks do not merely leak surplus carbon outward from large, well-lit trees but instead allocate it preferentially to plants experiencing the greatest physiological stress, suggesting that the distribution of resources tracks recipient need rather than the position or vigor of the source. Critics have countered that observed transfers might reflect nothing more than passive diffusion down concentration gradients from carbon-rich donors, a process that would favor whichever neighbors happen to lie nearest the most productive trees.

choices.text: When seedlings of varying sizes were connected to a single large donor tree, the quantity of carbon each seedling received correlated closely with how near it had been planted to that donor.

In a plot where some seedlings were artificially shaded to impair their photosynthesis, those shaded seedlings received markedly more carbon through the fungal network than equally distant unshaded seedlings did.
Large, well-lit trees were found to release substantial amounts of carbon into the fungal network throughout the growing season, far exceeding the amounts released by smaller, shaded trees.
Seedlings severed from the fungal network grew more slowly than connected seedlings, indicating that access to the network confers a measurable advantage on young plants.

explanation: Vasudevan claims the network directs carbon toward the neediest recipients rather than toward whoever is closest to a productive donor. Choice B isolates need by comparing shaded (stressed) seedlings against unshaded seedlings at equal distance, showing that greater stress, not proximity, predicts how much carbon arrives; this directly supports her proposal and rules out the critics' diffusion explanation. (A) supports the critics' proximity hypothesis, not Vasudevan's. (C) confirms that big trees are net sources but says nothing about how the carbon is allocated among recipients. (D) shows only that the network is beneficial, not that allocation tracks need.

targetedAbility: Select the detail that directly shows the lighthouse keeper relied on the seabirds' behavior to anticipate approaching storms.
prompt: Which quotation from Pugh's memoir most effectively illustrates the claim?
passage: In her memoir, lighthouse keeper Aderyn Pugh describes the seabirds that nested on the rocks beneath her station. She came to read their movements as a kind of forecast, learning that the birds behaved in distinctive ways before rough weather arrived. Long before instruments confirmed it, she trusted these signs to prepare the light and warn nearby fishing boats.

choices.text: "The rocks below my window were never silent, for the birds called to one another from dawn until the last gray light had gone."
"When the gulls abandoned the ledges all at once and wheeled inland, I knew to trim the lamp and signal the boats, for a gale was surely near."
"I had grown fond of the birds over my years at the station, and I missed their company in the seasons when they flew elsewhere."
"The lighthouse had stood upon that headland for nearly a century before I arrived to keep its light burning."

explanation: The claim is that Pugh used the birds' behavior to anticipate storms. Choice B shows exactly this: an observed behavior (gulls abandoning the ledges and wheeling inland) prompts her to prepare the light and warn boats because a gale is near. (A) describes the birds' noise, not weather prediction. (C) conveys affection for the birds. (D) gives the lighthouse's age. None of these connect bird behavior to forecasting storms.

targetedAbility: Identify the result showing that participants given a brief delay before deciding made more accurate judgments, supporting the claim that pausing improves accuracy.
prompt: Which finding, if true, would most directly support Delacroix's argument?
passage: Cognitive scientist Marcus Delacroix investigated whether briefly delaying a decision improves its accuracy. Participants viewed rapid sequences of images and judged whether each contained a specific target object. Some participants were required to wait two seconds before responding, while others responded as quickly as they wished. Delacroix argued that the imposed pause would allow perceptual information to accumulate more fully, yielding more accurate judgments without participants necessarily being aware of any improvement.

choices.text: Participants required to wait two seconds reported feeling more confident in their judgments than participants who responded immediately, though their confidence did not match their performance.
Participants required to wait two seconds before responding correctly identified the target object more often than participants who responded as quickly as they wished.
Participants who responded immediately completed the full sequence of trials in substantially less time than participants required to wait two seconds before each response.
Participants in both groups identified the target object more accurately when it appeared near the center of the image than when it appeared near the edges.

explanation: Delacroix argues that an imposed pause yields more accurate judgments. Choice B compares the two groups on accuracy and shows the delayed group identified targets correctly more often, directly supporting the claim. (A) addresses confidence, and Delacroix explicitly notes awareness is not the point. (C) concerns total time, not accuracy. (D) describes a position effect common to both groups, irrelevant to whether the pause helped.

targetedAbility: Identify the inscriptional evidence of officials adjusting and re-marking merchants' weights to a civic standard, supporting the historian's claim of centralized regulation over the weaker rival account of spontaneous private uniformity.
prompt: Which finding, if true, would most directly support Okonkwo's contention?
passage: Excavations at the ancient trading city of Sarmizet have yielded little direct documentation of how its crowded marketplace was governed, leaving historians to reconstruct its practices largely from the physical apparatus of commerce. Historian Lena Okonkwo contends that, despite this scarcity, the market was not a free-for-all of private bargaining but was instead subject to centralized regulation, with municipal officials actively enforcing a single civic standard on the merchants who traded there. Skeptics counter that the surviving artifacts are equally consistent with a looser arrangement in which individual traders gradually converged on whatever measures their partners would accept, so that any apparent uniformity reflects habit and mutual convenience rather than deliberate oversight imposed from above.

choices.text: Several merchants' weights recovered from across the marketplace cluster closely around a single value, departing from one another by only minor amounts despite having been used at stalls situated far apart within the excavated commercial district.

A number of the marketplace weights show signs of having been filed down or had metal plugs added after manufacture, and each such weight then matched a master measure that officials are recorded as having kept in a civic building.

Account tablets kept privately by traders at Sarmizet consistently recorded their transactions in the same units of measure, indicating that merchants throughout the marketplace had come to share a single commercial vocabulary over time.

Goods recovered at Sarmizet had originated in many distant regions, indicating that the marketplace functioned as a hub drawing merchants from across an extensive network whose members would have needed compatible measures to trade.
